title: Franklin Covey Co. Q2 2026 Earnings Call Summary
content:
Franklin Covey Co. Q2 2026 Earnings Call Summary - Moby

Strategic Performance Drivers

Management attributes the 7% invoice growth in Enterprise North America to the successful execution of a go-to-market transformation implemented last year, shifting focus toward solution-based selling. The 10% invoice growth in North America (excluding government) was driven by strong new logo acquisition and one of the highest revenue retention levels in recent periods, fueled by existing client expansion. Performance in the government sector was negatively impacted by disruptions and spending reductions at the federal level, though this was offset by broad-based commercial demand. Management views AI as a catalyst that increases the 'premium on human leadership,' positioning their behavior-change model as a necessary partner for technical transformations. The Education division's 16% revenue growth was driven by increased demand for 'Leader in Me' materials and services, despite the expiration of COVID-related ESSER funding. Strategic positioning focuses on being a 'performance partner' rather than a software provider, with 62% of revenue now under multiyear contracts to ensure long-term partnership stability.

Outlook and Strategic Assumptions

Fiscal 2026 is characterized as a 'year of execution,' with strong invoice growth expected to build a foundation for accelerated reported revenue and EBITDA in fiscal 2027. Management reaffirmed full-year adjusted EBITDA guidance of $28 million to $33 million, assuming 60% to 65% of that total will be generated in the fourth quarter due to Education seasonality. The company expects to achieve approximately 1 percentage point of operating leverage improvement annually, targeting a long-term adjusted EBITDA margin of 20%. Future growth assumptions rely on deep penetration of existing clients, where current solutions reach only 5% to 10% of the addressable employee population. Guidance for fiscal 2027 will be provided in the fall of 2026 following a summer planning cycle and an update to the five-year strategic plan.

Operational Adjustments and Risks

The company incurred $1.5 million in restructuring costs during the quarter, primarily related to severance and efforts to streamline operations for better operating leverage. A $0.5 million increase in building exit costs was noted, primarily consisting of legal expenses related to footprint optimization. Management highlighted the expiration of federal ESSER funds as a headwind for the Education business, though they believe private funding partners and state-level deals are mitigating this impact. Foreign exchange rates provided a $0.7 million favorable impact on consolidated revenue and a $0.2 million benefit to adjusted EBITDA.

Story Continues

Q&A Session Highlights

Sustainability of invoice growth and margin expansion targets

Our analysts just identified a stock with the potential to be the next Nvidia. Tell us how you invest and we'll show you why it's our #1 pick. Tap here.

Management confirmed that two consecutive quarters of 7% invoice growth in North America represent a sustainable trend rather than an isolated data point. The 20% adjusted EBITDA margin remains a firm long-term target, intended to be reached through a permanent reset of the cost structure and increased sales force productivity.

Education division pipeline and macro headwinds

The pipeline for Education is at record levels, including three multimillion-dollar state-level opportunities and large district deals. While the expiration of COVID relief funds is a factor, management noted that 'funding partners' provide approximately $20 million annually to help schools implement programs.

AI transformation as a new revenue driver

Management is seeing traction in helping companies through the 'human side' of AI adoption, noting that technical shifts fail without leadership and change management. They highlighted a specific win with a large technology company using Franklin Covey to support a CEO-led shift to an AI-enabled operating model.

Capital allocation and aggressive share buybacks

The company utilized 130% of free cash flow over the last 12 quarters for share repurchases, reflecting management's view that the stock is undervalued. With $20 million remaining on the current authorization, they intend to remain 'disciplined stewards' while prioritizing liquidity and growth investments.

One stock. Nvidia-level potential. 30M+ investors trust Moby to find it first. Get the pick. Tap here.

title: Franklin Covey reaffirms FY2026 guidance of $265M-$275M revenue and $28M-$33M adjusted EBITDA as multiyear mix reaches 62%
content:
Earnings Call Insights: Franklin Covey (FC) Q2 2026

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* CEO Paul Walker said the company was “pleased with our results in Q2” and framed fiscal 2026 as “a year of execution and a return to growth,” highlighting that invoiced amounts “grew 5%,” driven by “7% growth in Enterprise North America, or 10% when excluding our government business,” plus “7% growth in Enterprise International.” 
 	* Walker tied the invoicing trend to the revenue recognition model, saying, “We expect invoice growth to remain strong through the balance of the year,” and added that the invoicing mix “positions us for accelerating reported revenue, adjusted EBITDA and cash flow in fiscal '27.”
 	* Walker emphasized Enterprise North America momentum and the durability of contracts: “our balance of deferred subscription revenue grew a very strong 16% year-over-year to $59 million,” and “the percentage of our revenue, which is contracted for multiyear periods increased to 62%.”
 	* CFO Jessica Betjemann reported, “Total second quarter reported revenue was $59.6 million,” and said the company saw “consolidated invoiced amounts grew by 5%, resulting in a 7% increase in deferred revenue at the end of the second quarter.”
 	* President of Education Division Michael Covey said, “We have a really good pipeline of new opportunities, probably the best we've ever had,” and cited “3 state-level opportunities” that are “very large multimillion, multiyear deals.”

OUTLOOK

 	* Management reaffirmed full-year guidance. CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “We continue to affirm the revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for the year,” with “revenue guidance of $265 million to $275 million” and “adjusted EBITDA in the range of $28 million to $33 million.”
 	* Betjemann reiterated back-half seasonality and mix, stating, “We expect revenue to be slightly higher in Q4 compared to Q3, with approximately 50% to 55% of back half revenue in Q4,” and “For adjusted EBITDA, we expect approximately 60% to 65% to be generated in the fourth quarter.”
 	* Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained the same annual revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges, while continuing to emphasize invoiced-amount strength as setting up fiscal 2027 acceleration; CEO Paul Walker reiterated, “we remain confident in achieving our full year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance.”

FINANCIAL RESULTS

 	* CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “Consolidated subscription and subscription services revenue recognized for the second quarter increased 3% to $50.9 million,” while “the total value of contracts signed in the second quarter grew 8% to $53.7 million.”
 	* Betjemann highlighted deferred revenue and unbilled deferred revenue as indicators of future recognition: “deferred revenue balance” was “$101.5 million,” and “unbilled deferred revenue contracted for the second quarter… increased 9% to $10.6 million,” with “the total balance… $64.9 million.”
 	* Profitability and costs were framed around operating leverage and prior actions. Betjemann reported gross margin of “75.9%,” SG&A of “$41.2 million” (down from “$43.7 million”), and “Adjusted EBITDA… $4.1 million.”
 	* Betjemann said the company “incurred $1.5 million in expense for this restructuring activity” and reported “a net loss of $2 million.”
 	* Cash flow was a major quarter highlight: Betjemann reported operating cash flow for the first two quarters “increased 28% to $16.4 million” and “Free cash flow for the second quarter was $13.2 million.”

Q&A

 	* Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: asked whether management still expects North America/Enterprise invoiced growth to continue through the year; CEO Paul Walker answered, “Yes. We did. Yes.”
 	* Paris, Barrington Research: asked about the macro environment vs. last quarter; CEO Walker responded, “it's largely unchanged from what we saw a quarter ago,” and described it as “neutral… better than it was a year ago at this time.”
 	* Paris, Barrington Research: pressed on when longer-term guidance could be updated and whether 20% adjusted EBITDA margin remains a target; CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “in the fall and our Q4 call is when we're going to provide the guidance for our fiscal year 2027,” and added, “our goal is to have around 1 point improvement a year,” while CEO Walker said, “we do believe that, that 20%… is still a good number out there.”
 	* Jeff Martin, ROTH: asked what is driving Education strength; President Michael Covey cited “3 state-level opportunities,” “large district opportunities,” and said funding partners are “in the range of $20 million a year,” while noting uncertainty around “the Department of Education” and that “ESSER funds… are gone.”
 	* Nehal Chokshi, Northland: asked for drivers of free cash flow strength; CFO Betjemann answered, “a very strong positive swing in the net working capital… with regards to the collections on AR.”
 	* David Storms, Stonegate: asked if new logos are pilots and about AI-related use cases; CFO Betjemann said, “Very few of our new logos are pilots,” adding, “There's a ton of interest” in AI transformation and “it touches everyone.”

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts’ sentiment was slightly positive, with multiple questions reinforcing confidence in invoiced growth and cash generation; Nehal Chokshi called free cash flow “really strong” and highlighted buybacks as “a bold move,” while Alexander Paris framed “2 consecutive quarters of growth in invoiced amounts.”
 	* Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and stability; CEO Paul Walker said, “We feel great about our momentum,” and characterized the macro as “neutral,” while CFO Jessica Betjemann reiterated guidance and seasonality expectations.
 	* Compared with the previous quarter, management maintained the “year of execution” framing while speaking with more emphasis on conversion to fiscal 2027 results; CEO Walker again pointed to acceleration next year, and Q&A included more discussion of margin trajectory (“around 1 point improvement a year”) than in Q1.

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* Management kept fiscal 2026 guidance ranges unchanged quarter to quarter, with CFO Jessica Betjemann again affirming “$265 million to $275 million” revenue and “$28 million to $33 million” adjusted EBITDA.
 	* Enterprise North America continued to show two consecutive quarters of invoiced growth; in Q2, CEO Paul Walker cited “7% growth in Enterprise North America, or 10% when excluding our government business,” following Q1’s disclosure of “7%” growth and “13%” excluding government.
 	* Q2 placed heavier emphasis on cash flow and working-capital swing (including “Free cash flow… $13.2 million”), while Q1 featured a negative free cash flow quarter and heavier restructuring expense; the Q2 discussion also expanded on multiyear contract mix reaching “62%.”
 	* Analyst questioning shifted toward longer-term targets and timing of sharing multi-year plans; in Q2, Barrington asked when management would “update” longer-term guidance and whether “20% adjusted EBITDA margin” is still a reasonable target, whereas Q1 focused more on the initial inflection in North America invoicing and sales-force transformation impacts.

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Management cited government-related disruption in Enterprise North America: CEO Paul Walker said government business was “impacted by the disruption caused by a reduction in federal spending.”
 	* CFO Jessica Betjemann stressed “a disciplined view of the risks and opportunities ahead… in an uncertain macro environment,” tying mitigation to “cost reduction efforts including additional restructuring actions taken this quarter.”
 	* Education leadership flagged policy and funding uncertainties: President Michael Covey referenced “some uncertainty with what the Trump administration is going to do,” and said “ESSER funds… are gone,” alongside “declining enrollment in the public sector.”

FINAL TAKEAWAY

Management described Q2 as a continuation of fiscal 2026’s “year of execution,” pointing to 5% consolidated invoiced growth and increased multiyear contracting as building blocks for fiscal 2027 acceleration. Leadership reaffirmed full-year revenue guidance of $265 million to $275 million and adjusted EBITDA of $28 million to $33 million, while emphasizing a back-half weighted earnings profile and outlining cost actions, restructuring, and working-capital dynamics that drove $13.2 million of quarterly free cash flow.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/fc/earnings/transcripts]

title: Franklin Covey Q2 Earnings Call Highlights
content:
Franklin Covey logo

Key Points

Franklin Covey showed invoice momentum and a growing deferred revenue base—invoiced amounts rose 5% in Q2 (Enterprise NA +7% / +10% ex-government) and subscription deferred revenue was up 16%, with multi‑year contracts representing 62% of revenue, which management says positions the company for accelerating reported revenue, adjusted EBITDA and cash flow in fiscal 2027. Q2 financials were mixed: total revenue was flat at $59.6 million while Adjusted EBITDA nearly doubled to $4.1 million (+99%), but the company posted a $2.0 million net loss due mainly to $1.5 million in restructuring, higher share‑based compensation and building exit costs; Education revenue grew 16% while Enterprise reported revenue fell 4% despite higher invoiced amounts. Cash flow and buybacks strengthened the balance sheet—operating cash flow swung positive to $16.3 million in Q2 (YTD $16.4M) with free cash flow of $13.2 million, the company repurchased ~1.6 million shares for $28.1 million year‑to‑date, and liquidity stood above $76 million with about $20 million remaining on a $50 million repurchase authorization. Interested in Franklin Covey Company? Here are five stocks we like better.

Copper Cools After Record January—But This ETF Is a Buy-the-Dip Opportunity

Franklin Covey (NYSE:FC) executives said the company’s fiscal second quarter 2026 results showed continued momentum from its go-to-market changes in Enterprise North America, with invoiced amounts rising and deferred revenue building, while the Education Division delivered double-digit revenue growth.

Management highlights: invoice growth and expanding deferred revenue

CEO Paul Walker said the company was “pleased with our results in Q2,” noting that revenue and adjusted EBITDA grew year-over-year and came in above consensus expectations. Walker described fiscal 2026 as “a year of execution and a return to growth,” pointing to invoiced amounts that increased 5% in the quarter.

→ HP Inc. Stock Is Historically Cheap, but Can AI Change the Story?

Freeport-McMoRan's Rally Is Over—But the Bull Case Isn't

Walker said the invoiced growth was driven by a 7% increase in Enterprise North America invoiced amounts, or 10% excluding the government business, which he said was “impacted by the disruption caused by a reduction in federal spending.” He added that Enterprise International invoiced amounts grew 7%.

Because “a significant portion of invoice growth is recognized over time,” Walker said the company believes the invoiced momentum positions Franklin Covey for “accelerating reported revenue, Adjusted EBITDA and cash flow in fiscal 2027.”

Story Continues

→ Unity Soars After Axing a Business, Here's Why

Why Are Amphenol, Royal Caribbean, and Freeport Insiders Selling?

In Enterprise North America, Walker said results reflected strong new-logo activity and expansion within existing clients, contributing to “one of our highest revenue retention levels in recent periods.” He also highlighted that services bookings were up 9% year-to-date “as of this week.”

Walker and CFO Jessica Betjemann both pointed to a growing deferred revenue base. Walker said deferred subscription revenue rose 16% year-over-year and that multi-year contracts represented 62% of revenue. Betjemann later said billed deferred revenue in North America ended the quarter at $59.3 million, up 16% year-over-year.

Second quarter financial results

→ Surprising Beneficiaries of High Gas Prices: BJs and Costco

Betjemann reported total second quarter revenue of $59.6 million, flat year-over-year, as a 4% decline in Enterprise Division revenue was offset by a 16% increase in the Education Division. She said foreign exchange provided a $0.7 million favorable impact to consolidated revenue.

Subscription and subscription services revenue recognized increased 3% to $50.9 million. Betjemann said consolidated subscription and committed services invoiced amounts rose 16% to $39.3 million, and the total value of contracts signed increased 8% to $53.7 million, led by a 12% increase in the Enterprise Division.

Consolidated deferred revenue ended the quarter at $101.5 million, up 7% year-over-year. Unbilled deferred revenue contracted for the quarter increased 9% to $10.6 million, while the unbilled deferred revenue balance totaled $64.9 million, up 1% from the prior year.

Gross margin was 75.9%, compared to 76.7% a year earlier, which Betjemann attributed to higher amortization of capitalized curriculum expenses and a mix shift in services and products. SG&A expenses declined 6% to $41.2 million, reflecting reduced associate costs and prior cost reduction efforts.

Adjusted EBITDA was $4.1 million, up 99% year-over-year, with Betjemann citing stable revenue, gross margin, and lower SG&A. Foreign exchange benefited adjusted EBITDA by $0.2 million.

The company posted a net loss of $2.0 million, compared with a net loss of $1.1 million in the prior year. Betjemann attributed the change primarily to $1.5 million in restructuring expense (severance and related costs), higher share-based compensation expense, and increased building exit costs, partially offset by lower SG&A.

Segment performance: Enterprise softness in revenue, Education strength

Betjemann said the Enterprise Division contributed 70% of company revenue in the quarter, while the Education Division contributed 29%.

Enterprise Division invoiced amounts increased 7% to $52.0 million, while reported revenue declined 4% to $41.6 million. In Enterprise North America, invoiced amounts grew 7% to $42.7 million (10% excluding government). Reported revenue for the segment was $32.5 million, down 6% year-over-year, which Betjemann said primarily reflected lower subscription revenue recognized due to lower invoiced amounts and deferred revenues in the prior fiscal year.

Betjemann also discussed a shift tied to the company’s “solution selling” focus, where clients may commit upfront for predefined services delivered over time. She said approximately $3.5 million in invoiced amounts in the quarter came from such contractually committed predefined services, with revenue recognized upon delivery, and any unused days recognized at contract end.

Enterprise North America adjusted EBITDA rose to $5.9 million from $4.8 million, driven mainly by lower SG&A due to restructuring actions.

In Enterprise International, revenue was $9.2 million, up 1% year-over-year. Direct office revenue increased 7%, which Betjemann said was driven largely by foreign exchange benefits in France and China, while licensee revenue decreased 10%. Invoiced amounts for international direct offices grew 14%, with Betjemann noting that 6 points of that growth was due to foreign exchange. International segment adjusted EBITDA increased to $1.0 million from $0.5 million, helped by higher revenue and lower operating costs, including lower bad debt expense.

Education Division revenue rose 16% to $17.5 million, primarily reflecting “more than 300 additional training and coaching days” compared with last year, an additional symposium event, and increased school purchases of classroom and training materials, according to Betjemann. Education invoiced amounts were $8.5 million, slightly below $8.6 million a year ago, which she attributed partly to the timing of a large statewide deal expected to fall into the third and fourth quarters of fiscal 2026. Education subscription-related revenue increased 19% to $12.0 million. Education adjusted EBITDA was $0.4 million, compared with a loss of $0.3 million a year earlier.

Cash flow, buybacks, and liquidity

Betjemann reported operating cash flow of $16.4 million for the first two quarters of fiscal 2026, up 28% year-over-year, and said second quarter operating cash flow was $16.3 million versus negative $1.4 million in the prior-year quarter. Free cash flow was $13.2 million in Q2, compared to negative $3.6 million a year earlier.

On the Q&A, Betjemann attributed the free cash flow outperformance primarily to “a very strong positive swing in the net working capital,” especially improved accounts receivable collections.

The CFO said total liquidity was over $76 million at quarter end, including $13.7 million in cash and a fully available $62.5 million credit facility. She said Franklin Covey repurchased about 922,000 shares in the open market during the quarter for $16.5 million and completed a $20 million 10b5-1 purchase plan in January 2026. Year-to-date, the company repurchased nearly 1.6 million shares for $28.1 million. The company has a $50 million repurchase authorization, with $20 million remaining after completion of the two 10b5-1 plans.

Guidance reaffirmed; AI transformation positioned as demand driver

Betjemann reaffirmed fiscal 2026 guidance of $265 million to $275 million in revenue and $28 million to $33 million in adjusted EBITDA. She said the revenue outlook reflects “the lower deferred revenue generated in fiscal 2025 and the conversion lag of invoiced to reported revenue.”

For the second half, Betjemann said the company expects revenue to be “slightly higher in Q4 compared to Q3,” with roughly 50% to 55% of back-half revenue in Q4. For adjusted EBITDA, she said about 60% to 65% is expected in Q4, driven by education contributions and anticipated margin expansion from cost savings and operating leverage.

On the call, Walker said the macro environment was “largely unchanged” from the prior quarter, describing it as neutral and “more stable” than a year ago. Asked about longer-term targets, Betjemann said the company would evaluate whether to share longer-term direction as it updates its five-year plan during summer planning for fiscal 2027. Walker added that the company still views an adjusted EBITDA margin around 20% as “a good number out there,” stating that prior investments were not intended to “permanently reset the cost structure.”

Executives repeatedly framed AI-driven change as a tailwind for Franklin Covey’s leadership, execution, and behavior-change solutions. Walker said AI is “increasing the premium on human leadership and execution,” and emphasized the company’s model is built around “behavior change and collective action tied to real, measurable performance outcomes,” positioning it as a “performance and advisory partner rather than a software provider.”

In Q&A, CFO Betjemann said “very few of our new logos are pilots,” arguing it is “really hard to pilot a solution like ours.” Enterprise Division President Holly Procter said the company expects both new-logo growth and continued improvements in retention and expansion, citing traction from healthcare specialization and demand tied to helping companies through “AI transformation.”

In Education, division President Sean Covey said the segment has “probably the best [pipeline] we’ve ever had in terms of large opportunities,” including three state-level opportunities described as “very large multi-million, multi-year deals,” plus larger district opportunities. He cited continued support from funding partners “in the range of…$20 million a year,” and said the business is aligned with market needs such as raising test scores, teacher retention, and mental wellness, while acknowledging headwinds including uncertainty at the U.S. Department of Education and the expiration of ESSER funds.

About Franklin Covey (NYSE:FC)

Franklin Covey Co (NYSE:FC) is a global consulting and training firm specializing in performance improvement solutions for individuals and organizations. The company offers a range of services, including leadership development, productivity tools, execution frameworks and assessments designed to foster personal effectiveness and drive business results. Its flagship offerings integrate training workshops, digital resources and coaching to support clients in areas such as strategic planning, team productivity and change management.

The origins of Franklin Covey trace back to the merger in 1997 of Franklin Quest Co, founded in 1983 by Hyrum W.

The article "Franklin Covey Q2 Earnings Call Highlights" was originally published by MarketBeat.
